story:
Love, not fusion or storms, is the most powerful force I know—able to lift a heart or hollow it out. As a late-blooming senior stumbling through adolescence, I sat in the back of English class listening for any sign that I belonged. Chaucer and Shakespeare felt distant compared to the magnetic pull of Annie, the glowing girl by the window whose auburn hair and quiet grace set my mornings alight and my nights spinning with wishful fantasies. We were classmates yet strangers, paths unthreaded, eyes that never met, though not for lack of wanting.

Spring brought a spark. Miss Grant played the music of Camelot, and the legends leapt to life. In my mind I cast myself as Lancelot and Annie as Guinevere, a bold dream I could not match with action. I told my best friend Phil, the only one I trusted with my heart’s clumsy truth. He called it infatuation and warned me off, even suggesting I aim lower, as if affection were a line at a bakery and I’d always be last. I defended the light I saw in Annie—her laughter, her twinkling eyes—yet courage never came. Each day I wrote quiet entries in a bedside journal, small scenes that I hoped would add up to something brave. Instead, I walked the river by Route 80, sitting beneath a maple as ducks and carp drifted by, letting the murky water soothe the sting of rejection I feared. The harsh graffiti of our town—snide, crude, and careless—only made me more determined to believe in gentleness.

Leaving for college was my reveille. Away from the cramped rules of home, I learned to build a bridge toward tomorrow, even if I stumbled. The pursuit of love was a ballet I flubbed often: feet sore from falls, pride bruised by missteps. Ice and aspirin ease the body; nothing mends a broken heart so quickly. Still, I rose again, refusing to be fenced in by regret. Life moved fast. Experience taught me to read my own mind, to chart the roads I’d walked, to accept the blend of mirth and melancholy that colors a life.

In quiet moments I wondered about Annie. Where had she traveled? Had she crossed the place where love and passion meet and chosen well? Decades passed. At our thirty-year reunion, amid warm hugs and tempered ambitions, I finally walked up to her. “You might not remember me,” I began. She did. “You were that cute, quiet guy in the back,” she said, surprising me with a kindness I’d only imagined. I admitted my old crush; she confessed her youthful shyness, how she’d hidden inside a circle of friends and later chased the bright fruits of life without finding the man who made her feel whole.

The band played “Color My World,” and I asked her to dance. Held close, she whispered, “Why did it take so long?” In that sway, past and present braided into something tender. From long-ago longing, a gentle friendship bloomed. Life has taught me that people arrive for a reason, a season, or a lifetime. Annie was a short, sweet season—enough to color my world, and to make peace with the boy who once sat silent in the back row, dreaming of Camelot.
